title: Protecting Routes
intro: Your task manager has login working, but a curious user discovers they can access other people's tasks just by changing the URL. Imagine a building where the doors have name tags but no locks — anyone can walk into any room. Time to lock down your routes.

teach.title: Route Protection 101
teach.body: Protected routes are pages or API endpoints that require authentication. On the frontend, you redirect unauthenticated users to login. On the backend, middleware checks for a valid session or token before processing any request. Both layers are needed — frontend guards improve UX, but backend checks enforce real security.

teach.title: The Two Layers of Route Protection
teach.code:
Frontend: User visits /dashboard → Check if logged in → If not, redirect to /login

Backend: API receives GET /api/tasks → Middleware checks auth token → If invalid, return 401
                                      → If valid, only return THAT user's tasks
teach.annotation: Frontend protection is for UX (redirecting). Backend protection is for security (blocking). Never rely on frontend alone.

teach.title: Route Protection Is Like Airport Security
teach.body: The sign at the airport saying 'Authorized Personnel Only' is like frontend route protection — it stops honest people from wandering in. But the locked door with a badge scanner behind it is backend protection — it physically prevents access. If you only have the sign but no lock, anyone who ignores the sign walks right through.

Protected routes are the difference between an app you'd be embarrassed to launch and one you'd proudly charge money for. Lock down your routes, and you have a product that's ready for real paying customers.
Always enforce authorization on the server. Frontend route guards are a convenience for users, not a security measure. Every API endpoint must verify who is making the request.

start.narrative: A user named Alex discovers that by visiting /api/tasks?userId=42 they can see another user's tasks. Your API returns tasks based on the userId query parameter without checking who's logged in. How do you respond?

choices.text: Hide the userId parameter from the frontend URL so users can't see it
Add middleware that extracts the user ID from the auth token instead of the query parameter
Add a frontend check that prevents navigating to other users' pages

hide-param.narrative: Hiding the parameter doesn't help — anyone with browser dev tools can see the API calls and change the userId. Within hours, someone on Twitter posts about the vulnerability. You need real server-side protection.

choices.text: Implement middleware that derives the user ID from the verified auth token
Add rate limiting to slow down attackers and call it good

fix-middleware.narrative: You add auth middleware that reads the user ID from the verified JWT token, not the query parameter. GET /api/tasks now returns only the logged-in user's tasks. Time to look at the rest of the API.

choices.text: Open every other route file and add the same middleware where it's missing
Ship the GET fix and wait for users to report any other broken endpoints

frontend-only.narrative: The frontend check works in the browser, but a user uses curl to call your API directly, bypassing the frontend entirely. They can still access any user's data. The fix has to live on the server.

choices.text: Implement server-side middleware to validate auth on every API request
Block curl requests by checking the User-Agent header

endpoint-audit.narrative: Middleware is wired up on /api/tasks. You start auditing the remaining endpoints. PUT /api/tasks/:id authenticates the user but doesn't verify the task actually belongs to them. What do you do?

choices.text: Add an ownership check to every route that touches user-owned data — PUT, DELETE, GET-by-id, share, export
Just patch the PUT handler and move on; the rest can wait
Write a single requireOwnership(taskId, userId) helper and call it from every mutating endpoint

db-enforcement.narrative: Endpoint-level checks are in place, but a teammate points out that any future route that forgets the check still leaks data. You're using Supabase. How do you build a deeper safety net?

choices.text: Enable Row Level Security on the tasks table with a policy that restricts access to rows where user_id matches auth.uid()
Use the Supabase service role key on the server for everything and trust the app code to filter
App-level checks are enough — the database doesn't need to know about users

regression-tests.narrative: Auth is enforced at the route layer and the database layer. How do you make sure nobody silently breaks this protection in a future PR?

choices.text: Add integration tests that hit each endpoint as User A and assert they can't read or mutate User B's data
Add a 'check auth' line to your PR template and rely on reviewers to remember
Trust the team to do the right thing — tests are overkill for auth

excellent-end.narrative: Your task manager now derives user identity from the auth token, checks ownership on every mutating endpoint, enforces row-level security at the database, and has tests that prove unauthorized access returns 403. The original IDOR bug is closed and any regression will be caught before it ships.
excellent-end.feedback: This is layered authorization done right: middleware for who you are, endpoint checks for what you can touch, RLS as the last line of defense, and tests to keep all three honest. One of those layers will save you the day someone forgets to add the others.

mediocre-end.narrative: Three months later, a new endpoint ships without an ownership check because the reviewer skimmed the PR template. The bug only surfaces when a user accidentally sees another user's data and emails support.
mediocre-end.feedback: Checklists in PR templates rely on attention. Tests don't get tired. Add at least one auth integration test per endpoint family and you remove human error from the equation.

weak-end.narrative: Without tests, your auth fix lasts exactly until the next refactor. Someone changes the middleware order, the ownership check stops running, and User B's tasks leak again. The same bug, twice, in three weeks.
weak-end.feedback: Untested security is theoretical security. The day a vendor library updates or someone refactors a route, your protections vanish silently. Tests are the only thing that turns a one-time fix into a permanent one.
